questions.question: Assertion (A): The length of a tangent drawn from an external point to a circle is always equal to the radius of the circle. Reason (R): A tangent to a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact.

steps: Check Assertion (A): The length of the tangent is given by $\sqrt{d^2 - r^2}$, where $d$ is the distance from the external point to the center. It is not necessarily equal to the radius.
Check Reason (R): This is a standard theorem in NCERT, which is true.
Conclusion: A is false, but R is true.
solution.derivation: $Length = \sqrt{d^2 - r^2} \neq r \text{ (Assertion is false)} \\ \text{Tangent} \perp \text{radius (Reason is true)}$

questions.question: Assertion (A): The number of tangents that can be drawn from a point inside a circle is zero. Reason (R): A tangent to a circle intersects the circle at only one point.

steps: Check Assertion (A): Any point inside the circle divides the plane such that no line passing through it can touch the circle at only one point.
Check Reason (R): By definition, a tangent intersects a circle at exactly one point.
Conclusion: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
solution.derivation: \text{If point is inside, all lines are secants. Hence 0 tangents.}

questions.question: Assertion (A): If the angle between two tangents drawn from a point $P$ to a circle of radius $r$ and center $O$ is $60^\circ$, then $OP = 2r$. Reason (R): The line segment joining the center to the external point bisects the angle between the two tangents.

steps: Check Assertion (A): In $\triangle OTP$, $\angle TPO = 30^\circ$ (half of $60^\circ$). $\sin(30^\circ) = r / OP \Rightarrow 1/2 = r / OP \Rightarrow OP = 2r$.
Check Reason (R): This is a standard property of tangents from an external point.
Conclusion: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

questions.question: Assertion (A): Two concentric circles have the same center. Reason (R): A chord of the larger circle which touches the smaller circle is bisected at the point of contact.

steps: Check Assertion (A): Definition of concentric circles is correct.
Check Reason (R): This is a property of chords and tangents, which is true.
Conclusion: While both are true, R does not explain why circles are called 'concentric'.
solution.derivation: \text{Concentric circles share a center. Assertion is true.} \\ \text{Chord property is true, but does not define concentricity.}

questions.question: Assertion (A): The length of the tangent from a point $A$ at a distance of $5 \text{ cm}$ from the centre of the circle of radius $3 \text{ cm}$ is $4 \text{ cm}$. Reason (R): The tangent at any point of a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact.

steps: Check Assertion (A): By Pythagoras theorem, $L^2 + 3^2 = 5^2 \Rightarrow L^2 = 25 - 9 = 16 \Rightarrow L = 4 \text{ cm}$.
Conclusion: Both are true and R justifies the use of Pythagoras theorem.
